video_title: How Crypto and TradFi Are Starting to Merge | Moulik Nagesh of Binance
gist: Binance macro research lead Moulik Nagesh argues this crypto cycle is defined by institutionalization and regulatory clarity that are structurally lowering volatility, and by a two-way convergence of crypto and TradFi rails — ETFs and tokenization pulling crypto onto TradFi rails, while stablecoins, tokenized assets, and L1 specialization onboard liquidity on-chain. He flags stablecoins (especially B2B payments) as the dominant real use case, the Clarity Act and yield as the key regulatory swing factor, and rising quantum and DeFi-exploit risks as new premia to be priced in.

insights.claim: Crypto's buyer base has shifted from retail-driven to institution-driven, making demand more mandate-based and structurally lowering volatility versus prior cycles' peak-to-trough swings.
insights.speaker: Moulik Nagesh (Binance macro research lead)
insights.so_what: Lower realized vol and stickier flows change the risk/return profile and option pricing for crypto; trend-following and vol-selling strategies may need recalibrating.
insights.quote: the size of demand uh a large proportion of it is coming in from institutional side

insights.claim: During the recent ~30-40% market drawdown, Bitcoin ETF assets under management had a much smaller marginal drop than spot price, evidencing that institutional ETF capital is stickier and less reactive than retail.
insights.speaker: Moulik Nagesh (Binance macro research lead)
insights.so_what: ETF AUM resilience in selloffs is a tradable signal that the marginal holder base has changed; dip-buying ETF holders dampen downside cascades.
insights.quote: we saw the market drop by around uh 30 or 40 odd percent. Um but at the same time um the kind of the asset under management for ETFs uh had a much kind of smaller marginal based drop

insights.claim: Bitcoin dominance is rising because institutions prefer the harder, more mature large-cap asset, while reactiveness and speculation have concentrated into altcoins.
insights.speaker: Moulik Nagesh (Binance macro research lead)
insights.so_what: Persistent BTC dominance argues against a broad indiscriminate alt-season; alt exposure should be selective and fundamentals-driven.

insights.claim: Altcoin valuation is undergoing a 'return to fundamentals' and has become far more selective than prior narrative-driven cycles, which Nagesh frames as the key altcoin focus point for the rest of the year.
insights.speaker: Moulik Nagesh (Binance macro research lead)
insights.so_what: Favor altcoins with real fee/revenue and value accrual over narrative tokens; the dispersion between 'fundamental' and 'narrative' alts should widen.
insights.quote: there's a massive kind of drive towards a return toward fundamentals in terms of how we value altcoins

themes.theme: Bitcoin as a maturing macro asset class

insights.claim: Bitcoin is being reframed from a speculative/inflation-hedge narrative into a macro asset that provides enhanced risk-adjusted returns with regime-dependent correlation.
insights.speaker: Moulik Nagesh (Binance macro research lead)
insights.so_what: Treating BTC as a regime-dependent risk-adjusted return enhancer (not a constant hedge) changes how it should be sized and timed in a portfolio.
insights.quote: Bitcoin being this sort of macrobased asset that provides enhanced risk adjusted returns um and it correlation is very much regime dependent

insights.claim: Asset managers are driving toward roughly a 2.5% Bitcoin inclusion in the traditional 60/40 portfolio.
insights.speaker: Moulik Nagesh (Binance macro research lead)
insights.so_what: A ~2.5% allocation across large 60/40 mandates implies a sizable structural bid; quantify the addressable AUM to gauge incremental demand.
insights.quote: there's this drive for a 2.5% inclusion or so so on and we're seeing that drive really much um kind of come and take effect from various different kind of asset managers

insights.claim: Bitcoin behaves as a short-term risk-off reactor during geopolitical stress but acts as a semi-diversifier versus equities over longer horizons due to its distinct supply/cycle structure.
insights.speaker: Moulik Nagesh (Binance macro research lead)
insights.so_what: Horizon matters: short-term BTC won't hedge an equity shock, but it can still add long-run diversification — relevant for hedging vs. allocation decisions.

insights.claim: Bitcoin has never operated through a high-interest-rate or geopolitically volatile regime before, so its asset-class role remains unproven and its correlation profile will keep evolving with the next macro regime.
insights.speaker: Moulik Nagesh (Binance macro research lead)
insights.so_what: Backtests of BTC as a hedge are built on a zero-rate sample; don't over-trust historical correlation stats when sizing for a new rate regime.
insights.quote: We haven't kind of seen Bitcoin where we've sort of had like high interest rate environment. it was always where interest was pretty much uh near kind of like zero

themes.theme: ETF expansion up the risk curve

insights.claim: After Bitcoin and Ethereum ETFs, Solana ETFs have been approved and there is a push for a BNB ETF, with ETF-ization spreading to L1s that have strong value accrual, revenue, utility and fees.
insights.speaker: Moulik Nagesh (Binance macro research lead)
insights.so_what: L1 tokens with real fee revenue are the next ETF candidates; front-running ETF approval of revenue-generating L1s could capture an institutional access premium.
insights.quote: we've already seen kind of Salana based ETFs being approved. Um there's kind of a push for BNB based as well

insights.claim: Institutions accumulating L1 native tokens for ETFs evaluate them on fundamentals — value accrual, network activity, fee collection (revenue), and supply-side token emissions/inflation.
insights.speaker: Moulik Nagesh (Binance macro research lead)
insights.so_what: Screen L1 tokens like equities: fee revenue and net emissions (supply dilution) become the institutional valuation inputs to watch.

insights.claim: Institutional token valuation frameworks are still nascent, so expect more token-level investor relations, transparency, and standardized classifications of how tokens accrue value going forward.
insights.speaker: Moulik Nagesh (Binance macro research lead)
insights.so_what: Protocols that build TradFi-style IR and disclosure early may win the institutional allocation race; a coming standardization wave for token valuation.

insights.claim: General-purpose L1s are consolidating into specialized roles: Ethereum as the settlement/security/liquidity layer, BNB Chain as a retail distribution funnel from CEX to DeFi, and Solana as a high-throughput trading/UX layer.
insights.speaker: Moulik Nagesh (Binance macro research lead)
insights.so_what: Bet on chains that own a defensible niche rather than 'ETH-killer' generalists; valuation should hinge on the specialized flow each chain captures.
insights.quote: Ethereum, I think that's now naturally looked at more as a settlement based layer... BNB chain... providing a very good funnel for retail... Salana... towards just like trading uh volume and then uh throughput

insights.claim: Application-specific and specialized chains are emerging — Hyperliquid's own chain, Unichain, and stablecoin-focused chains like Arc and Tempo — confirming the move away from general-purpose L1s.
insights.speaker: Moulik Nagesh (Binance macro research lead)
insights.so_what: App-specific and stablecoin chains are a distinct emerging investable category; watch Arc/Tempo and Unichain as proxies for the specialization thesis.
insights.quote: whether it's hyperl with their own chain um we have kind of uni chain on that side and then uh more recently stable coinbased change uh in terms of like arc and and tempo

insights.claim: Binance/BNB Chain's strongest moat is the flow between centralized-exchange and decentralized-exchange liquidity, with Binance Alpha giving early asset and price discovery for tokens before they hit centralized exchanges.
insights.speaker: Moulik Nagesh (Binance macro research lead)
insights.so_what: A CEX-DEX distribution funnel is an underrated L1 moat; BNB's value accrual may track Binance's ability to route liquidity and discovery to its chain.
insights.quote: the strongest moat there... is sort of the flow between a centralized exchange liquidity as well as decentralized exchange

insights.claim: Fragmentation and cross-chain bridges create poor UX and security risk, which combined with liquidity gravitating to fewer venues makes consolidation the natural endpoint for the L1 landscape over 3-5 years.
insights.speaker: Moulik Nagesh (Binance macro research lead)
insights.so_what: Expect liquidity (and value) to concentrate in fewer specialized chains; bridge-dependent and long-tail generalist L1s are structurally disadvantaged.

insights.claim: Convergence is happening from both directions: crypto built on TradFi rails (ETFs, yield-on-Bitcoin products, crypto-backed mortgages) and TradFi built on crypto rails (stablecoins, payment rails, tokenization).
insights.speaker: Moulik Nagesh (Binance macro research lead)
insights.so_what: Map opportunities on both vectors; crypto-backed mortgages and yield-on-BTC products are early TradFi-rail use cases to watch.
insights.quote: crypto being built on traded rails and then we're also seeing being built on crypto rails

insights.claim: Tokenization is expanding into on-chain equities, commodities, and notably pre-IPO/private-market tokenized assets, giving retail-accessible exposure to previously gated private markets.
insights.speaker: Moulik Nagesh (Binance macro research lead)
insights.so_what: Tokenized pre-IPO/private-market access is a new, fast-growing product frontier; an opening for platforms that can source and structure private deals on-chain.
insights.quote: a space which very kind of new and and recent is um preipo based um tokenized assets um so providing exposure towards private market based assets

insights.claim: A financial 'super app' is emerging, led by neobanks, fintechs, and crypto exchanges because they are best positioned across payments and multiple asset classes, while traditional institutions lag waiting for regulation.
insights.speaker: Moulik Nagesh (Binance macro research lead)
insights.so_what: Neobanks/fintechs and crypto exchanges (not legacy banks) are the likely super-app winners; position toward platforms aggregating payments plus multi-asset access.
insights.quote: the advent of a super app uh emerge... driven by a lot of kind of near banks, fintex as well as cryptobased exchanges

insights.claim: Convergence is access- and UX-based, not valuation convergence — tokens and equities will remain valued by fundamentally different mechanisms even when held in the same app.
insights.speaker: Moulik Nagesh (Binance macro research lead)
insights.so_what: Don't assume tokenized assets get re-rated like equities; the valuation gap (tokenomics vs. cash flows) persists and is itself a source of edge.
insights.quote: this is all very much an access based convergence as well as a UX based one... when it comes towards the asset classes itself um they will remain different

insights.claim: Crypto is driving convergence faster than TradFi (crypto-to-TradFi) because it operates more quickly and nimbly, evidenced by strong growth in stablecoins, tokenization, and crypto exchanges listing TradFi assets.
insights.speaker: Moulik Nagesh (Binance macro research lead)
insights.so_what: First-mover advantage in tokenized-TradFi-asset listings favors crypto-native venues; volume is already migrating there.
insights.quote: crypto can operate a lot more let's say quicker and and more nimly um it's been crypto to trady

insights.claim: Crypto exchanges listing tokenized TradFi assets generate strong volume by offering access during off-market and weekend hours, letting investors react to news and geopolitics outside traditional sessions.
insights.speaker: Moulik Nagesh (Binance macro research lead)
insights.so_what: Weekend/off-hours tokenized-equity trading is a real volume driver; an edge for venues offering 24/7 access to TradFi assets.

insights.claim: Crypto and TradFi will split convergence by their existing edge: crypto leads consumer-to-consumer and merchant payments, while TradFi leads B2B and corporate-treasury use cases.
insights.speaker: Moulik Nagesh (Binance macro research lead)
insights.so_what: Segment exposure by use case: crypto-native players for retail/merchant payments, TradFi rails for B2B/treasury — they won't simply out-execute each other.

themes.theme: Stablecoins as the dominant real use case

insights.claim: Stablecoins are the strongest, most front-and-center crypto use case and the primary bridge between TradFi and crypto rails, with payments (B2B and consumer-to-business) the biggest growth area.
insights.speaker: Moulik Nagesh (Binance macro research lead)
insights.so_what: Stablecoin payment rails are the clearest 'real' adoption vector; exposure to issuers and payment infrastructure captures the highest-conviction use case.
insights.quote: The biggest one which I think is is very kind of front and center is stable coins

insights.claim: Stablecoin trading-pair volume still dominates usage, so investors must use adjusted-volume metrics that strip out trading to isolate genuine payment, B2B, commerce, and cross-border remittance flows.
insights.speaker: Moulik Nagesh (Binance macro research lead)
insights.so_what: Headline stablecoin volume is misleading; track adjusted/use-case-segmented volume to separate real adoption signal from trading noise.
insights.quote: the the trading volume side um, that dominates stable coin usage... there's a lot more kind of metrics that are coming about in terms of adjusted um volume

insights.claim: Stablecoin use cases differ sharply by market: in the US they're financial infrastructure for payment-processing efficiency, while in emerging markets (notably South America) they drive payments and 'digital dollarization' to hedge high local-currency inflation.
insights.speaker: Moulik Nagesh (Binance macro research lead)
insights.so_what: Geographic segmentation matters: EM digital-dollarization demand is a distinct, inflation-driven growth driver separate from US institutional adoption.
insights.quote: in some markets is also seen as sort of um digital dollarization uh... especially in South American countries seen as as a way to get exposure to the dollar

insights.claim: The key second-order effect of stablecoin payment/treasury adoption is onboarding liquidity on-chain, which then naturally pulls that liquidity toward yield, DeFi, and broader crypto markets.
insights.speaker: Moulik Nagesh (Binance macro research lead)
insights.so_what: Stablecoin growth is a leading indicator of future DeFi/crypto demand; payment liquidity is a funnel into on-chain yield and asset markets.
insights.quote: it's it's essentially onboarding a lot of liquidity on chain... more propensity for a lot of this like new liquidity that's come from let's say payment based rles to eventually flow towards um cryptobased markets

insights.claim: Hyperliquid and crypto perpetual markets demonstrated faster risk-off repricing during recent geopolitical tension than traditional markets, with that move later reflected in standard asset classes.
insights.speaker: Moulik Nagesh (Binance macro research lead)
insights.so_what: 24/7 on-chain perps can lead TradFi repricing during off-hours shocks; they're a real-time sentiment/forward indicator for weekend gap risk.

insights.claim: Stock exchanges including NASDAQ (and S&P discussing 24/7 on-chain perps) are moving toward tokenizing equities and offering broader 24/7 access, adopting the always-on model crypto pioneered.
insights.speaker: Moulik Nagesh (Binance macro research lead)
insights.so_what: TradFi exchanges adopting tokenization/24-7 validates crypto infrastructure; watch NASDAQ tokenization moves as a convergence catalyst.
insights.quote: We're seeing um NASDAQ and kind of um the stock exchanges themselves wanting to tokenize

insights.claim: The main risk of fast 24/7 tokenized markets is immature price discovery — tokenized on-chain prices can diverge from equity-market prices until more liquidity enters.
insights.speaker: Moulik Nagesh (Binance macro research lead)
insights.so_what: Tokenized-vs-spot price gaps create arbitrage but also execution risk; thin on-chain liquidity is the binding constraint on these markets today.
insights.quote: the main risk tends to be price discovery and liquidity... there's still some disparities in terms of... the price... quoted on uh especially onchain like tokeniz based... versus uh what ends up being on... equity based markets

insights.claim: AI agents are an emerging demand layer that rely on crypto rails because traditional bank accounts' KYC/regulatory requirements make it hard for agents to transact, so stablecoins and wallet-based products become their execution infrastructure.
insights.speaker: Moulik Nagesh (Binance macro research lead)
insights.so_what: The agentic economy is a structural new demand source for stablecoins/wallet infra; an investable theme as agent adoption scales.
insights.quote: the use case of stable coins and and wallet based um products come into play where agents can essentially execute

insights.claim: Agent adoption follows a phased path — first a co-pilot/assist phase across commerce, trading, and business use cases, then a transaction-execution phase that drives real stablecoin/payment-rail usage.
insights.speaker: Moulik Nagesh (Binance macro research lead)
insights.so_what: Stablecoin demand from agents is back-loaded to the execution phase; track the co-pilot-to-execution transition as the real adoption trigger.

insights.claim: Tokenizing debt and private credit mirrors tokenizing equities — needing issuer frameworks, off-chain-to-on-chain data rails, and security — but requires rebuilding institutions' legacy operating processes and integrations on-chain, a slow buildout.
insights.speaker: Moulik Nagesh (Binance macro research lead)
insights.so_what: On-chain private credit is early-stage infrastructure; the bottleneck is replicating legacy institutional processes, favoring infra builders over speculation.
insights.quote: thousands and thousands of legacy based integrations. So all of that will essentially need to be catered for

insights.claim: On-chain borrowing/money markets are less mature than stablecoin holding, and as rates rose the spread between DeFi yield and the risk-free Treasury rate compressed, with strong demand for yield but weak demand for on-chain borrowing.
insights.speaker: Moulik Nagesh (Binance macro research lead)
insights.so_what: Compressed DeFi-vs-Treasury spread plus weak borrow demand signals thin on-chain credit demand; lending-protocol revenue may stay pressured until rates fall or use cases mature.
insights.quote: the risk-f free rate which is treasury based yield um and these days given interest rates have kind of essentially gone up um that that spread has has decreased over time

insights.claim: Regulatory trends are the single biggest crypto catalyst this year, with the Clarity Act the current focus — debated mainly around whether stablecoin issuers and crypto entities can pass yield through to retail users.
insights.speaker: Moulik Nagesh (Binance macro research lead)
insights.so_what: Stablecoin yield pass-through is the regulatory swing factor; a favorable Clarity Act outcome could unlock a major new retail yield demand wave.
insights.quote: the current one in focus is is... the clarity act. Um that's something which uh currently is in kind of undergoing debate mainly from a yield perspective

insights.claim: The GENIUS Act's passage last year was the first major catalyst that drove stablecoin adoption and volume, and Nagesh expects tokenization and other use cases to follow the same regulation-then-adoption pattern.
insights.speaker: Moulik Nagesh (Binance macro research lead)
insights.so_what: Use regulatory passage as a forward catalyst playbook: GENIUS → stablecoins suggests the next act could similarly inflect tokenization adoption.
insights.quote: once the genius act was essentially uh approved... that's essentially the first major catalyst that drove the stable coin uh based kind of adoption and volume

insights.claim: Regulatory passage repricing is immediate in markets (and in crypto-rail public equities) but real product adoption lags as a structured, use-case-dependent buildout — so the 'rails free-flowing next day' expectation is wrong.
insights.speaker: Moulik Nagesh (Binance macro research lead)
insights.so_what: Trade the price reaction to regulatory news quickly, but the adoption ramp is a 1-2 year structured process — separate the pricing trade from the fundamentals trade.
insights.quote: it wouldn't be kind of immediate per se, but I I think we'll see it as a natural kind of progression... what is probably more immediate in terms of reaction is is what will get reflected in terms of... how crypto markets are valued

themes.theme: Emerging risks to price in (quantum & DeFi exploits)

insights.claim: Quantum risk has accelerated as an industry concern this year, partly due to a Google research paper, and a 'quantum-resistance progress' risk premium will start being priced into Bitcoin and general-purpose L1s.
insights.speaker: Moulik Nagesh (Binance macro research lead)
insights.so_what: Chains' progress toward quantum resistance may become a valuation differentiator; lagging chains could carry a discount as the premium gets priced.
insights.quote: this risk will start being priced in uh towards how far um a particular uh whether it's Bitcoin or um a layer one... they are in the progression of becoming quantum resistant

insights.claim: April saw one of the largest months of DeFi exploits since early last year by incident count, triggering on-chain liquidity outflows, falling total value locked, and price hits to DeFi assets.
insights.speaker: Moulik Nagesh (Binance macro research lead)
insights.so_what: Exploit-driven TVL outflows directly hit DeFi token prices; security-incident frequency is a tradable risk signal for the DeFi sector.
insights.quote: we've seen kind of April um have one of the largest kind of like uh months in terms of um exploits um since um we say early last year

insights.claim: The industry is debating whether DeFi yield correctly prices exploit risk, with proposed mitigations including market-based recovery funds, higher risk-adjusted yields, and on-chain insurance.
insights.speaker: Moulik Nagesh (Binance macro research lead)
insights.so_what: On-chain insurance and recovery-fund models are an emerging investable response to DeFi risk; current DeFi yields may be underpricing tail risk.
insights.quote: whether uh you know defy yield itself um it's not pricing this risk uh correctly and whether yield needs to maybe more attractive

themes.theme: Token valuation gap & what TradFi misunderstands

insights.claim: Nagesh's key message to TradFi is that tokens are valued on fundamentally different demand- and supply-side dynamics (tokenomics, emissions, protocol role) than equities' cash-flow/earnings-call metrics, and investors should keep tokenomics front and center.
insights.speaker: Moulik Nagesh (Binance macro research lead)
insights.so_what: Applying equity cash-flow valuation to tokens is a category error; supply-side emissions and protocol token-role analysis are essential for valuing crypto assets.
insights.quote: keep tokconomics front and center... understanding the role of a token uh in a particular protocol... value cruel is essentially done... both from a demand side as well as a supply side
